name: differentiated instruction tools
top_issues: C9 (Standards Alignment) scores 1: A lesson-design article covering differentiation, formative assessment, adaptive practice, and inclusivity never once mentions Common Core, NGSS, ISTE, IDEA, or any state framework — this is a glaring omission for a teacher audience who must justify tool adoption to administrators using standards language.

C10 (Wayground Value Prop) scores 2: The only Wayground mention is a one-sentence closer — 'Wayground offers a supplemental platform that adapts to your classroom' — with zero feature specificity. The article spends hundreds of words describing ideal tool features (read-aloud, adaptive difficulty, color-coded dashboards) without ever showing that Wayground delivers any of them by name.

C2 (Opening Conciseness) scores 2: The article opens with a rhetorical question ('What differentiated instruction tools help teachers...') before the actual content begins — a classic AI-opener pattern that delays value. The key takeaways box above it is strong, but the body intro undercuts the opening momentum.
positive_signals: The comparison table (C4) is genuinely classroom-ready — a teacher could screenshot it during a curriculum night or PD session; the categories, data columns, and workflow-fit column are exactly the kind of side-by-side framing busy teachers need.

Grade-band anchoring is used well in the body examples (7th-grade math at Lincoln Middle, 6th-grade science at Riverside Elementary, 5th-grade exit ticket path) — these concrete scenarios give the advice real credibility rather than floating in the abstract.
narrative: This article has strong bones — the comparison table, concrete grade-band scenarios, and teacher-agency framing are genuinely useful — but two automatic REVISE triggers prevent it from being classroom-ready: standards alignment is entirely absent (a critical gap for any teacher who needs to pitch tool adoption to a department head or align to an IEP mandate), and Wayground's own value proposition is reduced to a single vague closing sentence after an article that spends 1,000+ words describing exactly what a great differentiation platform should do. The opening rhetorical question also needs to be cut and replaced with the direct, fact-first framing the key-takeaways box already models. Fix those three issues — add a brief standards callout (ISTE 5, IDEA accommodation requirements, or relevant CCSS anchor standards), replace the closing Wayground sentence with one specific feature tied to a use case already described in the article, and rewrite the opening sentence — and this moves cleanly into PASS territory.
